moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the Polish Opening, an uncommon but strategically rich setup where you immediately seize space on the queenside with your b-pawn. This move prepares Bb2, allowing your bishop to control the long diagonal (a1-h8) and support your center. It's a surprising choice that puts your opponent immediately out of book—many players prepare for 1.e4 or 1.d4, so you gain a practical advantage while developing a coherent plan around queenside expansion and fianchetto ideas.

Black develops naturally and prepares to establish the King's Indian setup, contesting your central plans. By placing the knight on f6 before committing the d-pawn, Black keeps flexibility—they can play either d6 or d5 depending on how your opening unfolds. This move also eyes the e4 square, ready to challenge any center you build.

Now your fianchettoed bishop takes control of the long diagonal, aiming at g7 and supporting your future center on c4 and d4. This bishop pair (Bb2 and your future light-squared bishop) will become powerful assets in the middlegame, especially if you can plant central pawns. Notice that Bb2 also subtly supports your c4 pawn and prevents Black's pieces from easily accessing those squares.

Black confirms their King's Indian setup, preparing to fianchetto their own bishop on g7 to mirror your space control and defend the long diagonal. This pawn move creates a solid structure around Black's kingside and allows the bishop on g7 to become an active defender. By fianchettoing, Black accepts your central space but commits to a flexible, counterattacking setup where they'll target d4 and e3 later.

You seize the initiative in the center, staking a claim on c4 and preparing d4 to establish a classical pawn center. This move follows naturally from Bb2, as your bishop now supports this pawn. By playing c4 before d4, you're following the Sokolsky Attack plan: gaining space methodically, maintaining piece flexibility, and preventing Black from easily contesting your central setup—if they challenge with d5, c4xd5 opens lines favorably.

Black completes their fianchetto setup, placing the bishop on g7 where it scrutinizes your center and the long diagonal. This bishop is Black's key piece in a King's Indian structure—it exerts pressure on c3, supports counterplay with ideas like d5 or Nbd7-c5, and prepares to support the d6 pawn once Black solidifies the center. The bishop on g7 will be a constant thorn in your side, so your d4 pawn must be well-supported.

You solidify your center and prepare d4, keeping everything under control with pawns on e3 and c4. Playing e3 is more solid than an immediate d4, allowing you to support that pawn with your e-pawn and prevent Black's pieces from invading d3. This measured approach emphasizes piece placement and space control rather than aggressive expansion—you're building a patient, positional squeeze where Black's counterplay will be limited.

Black establishes a solid pawn on d6, supporting their fianchettoed bishop and preparing to potentially challenge your center with ...e5 or maintain flexibility with a future ...d5. This move completes Black's typical King's Indian structure and prepares the rook to move along the d-file. Playing d6 also avoids committing to ...e6 (which would block the bishop on g7), keeping Black's position resilient and flexible.

You develop your kingside with a logical choice that supports your center (especially the e3 and d4 pawns), prepares to castle kingside, and controls the critical e5 and g5 squares. Nf3 is the classical third move for any player building a center—it improves your piece coordination and prevents Black from easily playing ...e5 without consequences. This knight will eventually support your center or reroute to d4 or e5 as the position demands.

Black castles kingside, tucking the king to safety and connecting the rooks—a logical choice that completes Black's setup. Castling also prepares Black to challenge your center with pieces, particularly moving the rooks to the center files and the queen to support counterplay. By castling now, Black is ready to resist your impending d4 push and mount counterattacking chances along the d-file and center.

At last, you complete your classical center with d4, establishing pawns on c4 and d4 that control the core squares and cramp Black's position. This move is the culmination of your Sokolsky plan—now your center is set, your pieces are developed, and Black must decide how to challenge you. The d4 pawn is supported by your c4 pawn and e3 pawn, and Black's fianchettoed bishop on g7 cannot immediately neutralize it; this gives you a slight space advantage and the initiative heading into the middlegame.
